title: Debugging Test Failures
intro: Your tests are failing and you need to figure out why. Is it a real bug or a bad test? Swipe right on good debugging practices, left on bad ones.

teach.title: When Tests Fail
teach.body: A failing test means one of two things: your code has a bug, or your test is wrong. Before assuming it's a code bug, read the error message carefully, check if the test setup is correct, and verify the test expectations match the current requirements. Many 'bugs' are actually outdated tests.

teach.title: Reading a Test Failure
teach.code:
FAIL: 'should return tasks sorted by due date'
  Expected: ['Task A (Jan 1)', 'Task B (Jan 5)']
  Received: ['Task B (Jan 5)', 'Task A (Jan 1)']

This tells you: the sort order is reversed.
Check: Is the sort function wrong? Or did the test expect the wrong order?
teach.annotation: Always read the expected vs received values. They often tell you exactly what went wrong.

teach.body: Read the error message first — not the code. Most test failures tell you exactly what went wrong if you read the output carefully.

cards.content: When a test fails, read the error message and compare expected vs actual values before looking at code
cards.explanation: The error output usually tells you exactly what went wrong. Jumping into code before reading the error wastes time and leads you down wrong paths.

cards.content: If a test is failing, delete it and rewrite it from scratch
cards.explanation: Understand why it's failing first. The test might be correct and revealing a real bug. Deleting it could hide a legitimate problem.

cards.content: Run the failing test in isolation to rule out interference from other tests
cards.explanation: Tests sometimes fail because a previous test left behind dirty state (modified database, changed global variables). Running in isolation confirms if the test itself is broken or if it's an ordering issue.

cards.content: Mark flaky tests as 'skip' so they don't block the CI pipeline
cards.explanation: Skipping flaky tests is a temporary measure at best. Flaky tests often hide real bugs. Fix the root cause — usually timing issues, shared state, or brittle selectors.

cards.content: Check if recent code changes match what the test expects — the test might need updating for new requirements
cards.explanation: Requirements change. If you renamed a field from 'done' to 'completed', tests checking for 'done' will fail. That's not a bug — the test just needs to reflect the new contract.

cards.content: If tests pass locally but fail in CI, the tests are probably fine — just retry the CI build
cards.explanation: Different environments reveal real issues: timezone differences, missing environment variables, database state. Investigate the environment difference instead of blindly retrying.
